plainMeaning: Lord Śāntinātha — adored by gods, demon-lords, the moon and the sun, his smile dazzlingly white and his virtues supreme — is begged to grant the reciter calm, settled inner peace.
recitation: deva dāṇaviṁda caṁda sūravaṁda haṭṭha tuṭṭha jiṭṭha parama, laṭṭha ruva dhaṁta ruppa paṭṭaseya suddha niddha dhavala | daṁtapaṁti saṁti satti kitti mutti jutti gutti pavara, dittatea vaṁdadhea, savvaloa bhāvia ppabhāvaṇoa païsa me samāhiṁ

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. deva (from √div, 'to shine'); here first member of the long compound naming those who venerate the Jina.
wordByWord.notes: Subject of the implied veneration (vaṁda) governing the whole opening string.

wordByWord.etymology: dānava ('demon, titan') + iṁda < Skt. indra ('chief, lord'); Prakrit -nd- retained, intervocalic v softened.
wordByWord.notes: Even the asura-Indras bow to him; a stock pairing with deva to mean 'the whole of heaven and the netherworld'.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. candra > Pkt. caṁda (conjunct -ndr- > -nd-).
sūra (Skt. sūrya, 'sun') + vaṁda (Skt. vandita / vandya, 'bowed to, worthy of homage').
wordByWord.notes: vaṁda closes the list, so its sense ('revered, bowed to') governs the whole: revered by gods, demon-lords, moon and sun.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. hṛṣṭa > Pkt. haṭṭha (ṛ > a, -ṣṭ- > -ṭṭh-).
wordByWord.notes: May describe the joyful venerators or the serenely joyful Jina; the syntax allows both (see notes).

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. tuṣṭa > Pkt. tuṭṭha. Paired with haṭṭha as the common dvandva 'hṛṣṭa-tuṣṭa' (overjoyed).
Skt. jyeṣṭha ('eldest, chief') > Pkt. jeṭṭha/jiṭṭha.
wordByWord.notes: Epithet of the Jina: the senior-most, pre-eminent one.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. parama, superlative of para.
Pkt. laṭṭha ('charming, fine'; Sheth glosses manohara/sundara), commonly derived from Skt. laṣṭa.
wordByWord.notes: Begins the description of his radiant form.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. rūpa > Pkt. ruva (intervocalic p > v); laṭṭha-ruva = 'of beautiful form'.
Pkt. dhaṁta < Skt. dhmāta ('blown upon by fire, smelted'); per Cologne/Prakrit lexicon dhaṁta also = dhmāta.
wordByWord.notes: Qualifies ruppa: silver purified in fire — flawlessly bright.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. rūpya ('wrought silver') > Pkt. ruppa (Sheth: ruppa = cāndī).
wordByWord.notes: dhaṁta-ruppa: 'refined silver', the standard of pure whiteness for his teeth/form.

wordByWord.etymology: paṭṭa ('silk, fine woven cloth') + seya (Skt. sveta, 'white').
wordByWord.notes: Reading 'seya = sveta' (white) is adopted; a minority parse takes seya as sveda. Whiteness-imagery either way.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. śuddha > Pkt. suddha (ś > s, -ddh- retained).
Skt. snigdha ('smooth, oily-bright') > Pkt. niddha.
Skt. dhavala ('white, bright'); same in Pkt.
wordByWord.notes: Caps the run of whiteness epithets (silver, silk, pure, glossy, white).

wordByWord.etymology: danta ('tooth') + paṁti < Skt. paṅkti ('row, line'); -ṅkt- > -ṁti.
wordByWord.notes: The noun the whole white-imagery (silver, silk, pure, glossy, white) describes: his teeth gleam like refined silver.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. śānti ('peace') > Pkt. saṁti; here both the name Śāntinātha and the quality 'peace'.
wordByWord.notes: Pivot of the verse's name-play; also heads the following virtue-garland as 'peace'.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. śakti > Pkt. satti (-kt- > -tt-).
Skt. kīrti > Pkt. kitti (-rt- > -tt-).
Skt. mukti ('release') > Pkt. mutti.
wordByWord.notes: Some recensions read mutti = mūrti ('form'); 'liberation' best fits the abstract-virtue series.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. yukti ('fitness, method') > Pkt. jutti (y > j).
Skt. gupti ('guarding of mind, speech, body') > Pkt. gutti.
wordByWord.notes: Technical term: the three guptis of ascetic discipline.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. pravara ('most excellent') > Pkt. pavara.
wordByWord.notes: Closes the garland: foremost in peace, power, fame, liberation, propriety, restraint.

wordByWord.etymology: ditta < Skt. dīpta ('blazing') + tea < Skt. teja ('radiance, fiery glory').
vaṁda < Skt. vandya ('to be praised') + dhea < Skt. dhyeya ('to be meditated upon').
wordByWord.notes: He is at once the object of outward worship and inward contemplation.

wordByWord.gloss: all the worlds / the whole world
wordByWord.etymology: savva < Skt. sarva ('all') + loa < Skt. loka ('world'); intervocalic k elided.
Skt. bhāvita ('meditated upon, made present') > Pkt. bhāvia (-t- elided).

wordByWord.gloss: O you whose majestic power (is contemplated)
wordByWord.etymology: (p)pabhāvaṇa < Skt. prabhāvana / prabhāva ('majestic might, glory'); -oa is an Apabhraṁśa case/vocative ending; gemination ppa- by sandhi.
wordByWord.notes: savvaloa-bhāvia-ppabhāvaṇa: 'whose glory is meditated upon throughout all the worlds'.

wordByWord.etymology: imperative; from Skt. pradiśa ('grant, assign', √diś with pra-).
wordByWord.notes: Many recensions read 'pasia/pasiya' (Skt. prasīda, 'be gracious'); the book's reading પઇસ = 'grant' is followed here.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt./Pkt. me, enclitic dat./gen. of the 1st person.
Skt. samādhi > Pkt. samāhi (Sheth's Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo: samāhi = samādhi); accusative samāhiṁ, object of païsa.
wordByWord.notes: The verse's culminating petition: 'grant me samādhi'.

literalTranslation: Revered by gods, demon-lords, the moon and the sun; delighted, content, foremost, supreme — his row of teeth lovely in form, [white] as fire-refined silver and white silk, pure, glossy, dazzling white; pre-eminent in peace, power, fame, liberation, propriety, and self-restraint; of blazing splendour, worthy to be venerated and meditated upon; O you whose majesty is contemplated throughout all the worlds — grant me samādhi.
idiomaticTranslation: Gods and the lords of demons, the moon and the sun bow before you, foremost and supreme, who fill them with joy. Your smile gleams like fire-purified silver, like white silk — pure, lustrous, dazzlingly white. Pre-eminent in peace, in power, fame, liberation, right conduct and self-restraint, blazing with splendour, worthy of both worship and inward meditation, your majesty is held in mind across all the worlds. O Śānti, grant me settled, unshakable inner calm.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Turning from Ajita (praised just before) to Śānti, this stanza builds a single shimmering image and ends in a plea. The first half stacks honorifics — gods, asura-Indras, moon and sun all 'bow' (vaṁda) to him — then four epithets (joyful, content, foremost, supreme) that read both ways: he is supreme, and he makes his venerators glad. The poet's real interest is colour: laṭṭha-ruva opens a cascade of whiteness — dhaṁta-ruppa (silver smelted in fire), paṭṭa-seya (white silk), suddha, niddha, dhavala — all resolving onto daṁta-paṁti, his gleaming row of teeth. The Jina's very smile becomes a meditation on flawless purity. The second half pivots on saṁti: the syllable names Śāntinātha and simultaneously heads a garland of qualities he is 'foremost' (pavara) in — satti, kitti, mutti, jutti, gutti (power, fame, liberation, propriety, self-restraint). He is then dittatea (blazing) yet vaṁdadhea — fit for outward homage and inner dhyāna alike — and savvaloa-bhāvia-ppabhāvaṇa, contemplated everywhere. After all this splendour the verse asks for one quiet thing: païsa me samāhiṁ, 'grant me samādhi.' The movement from cosmic adoration to a single inward request is the stanza's whole point: the dazzling Jina is praised so that the reciter may receive composure of mind.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The closing petition frames the verse doctrinally. Samādhi — collected, equanimous absorption — is the practitioner's near-goal on the path to mukti, itself named here among his excellences. Gutti (gupti) is a precise ascetic term: the threefold guarding of mind, speech and body; pairing it with jutti (yukti, right method) and satti (śakti) sketches the disciplined energy that ripens into liberation. That the dazzling-white imagery culminates not in a boast but in a request for inner calm mirrors a core Jain conviction: contemplating the Jina's flawless purity (here, literally his radiant form) is a means of purifying one's own consciousness. vaṁdadhea makes this explicit — the Tīrthaṅkara is both vandya (to be worshipped) and dhyeya (to be meditated upon), and devotion matures into meditation.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited within the protective smaraṇa, the verse models how to use an image as a doorstep to stillness: the reciter dwells on the Jina's serene, silver-white form until the agitated mind settles, then voices the actual prayer — not for wealth or rescue but for samādhi. It teaches that the fruit sought from worship is composure; the dazzling description is upāya (means), the calm mind is the aim.

crossReferences.text: Logassa: 'samāhi-vara-muttamaṁ diṁtu' — 'may they grant the most excellent samādhi'
The three guptis (mano-, vacana-, kāya-gupti) underlying 'gutti'
Recurring refrain-petition 'pasiya/païsa me samāhiṁ' across the Ajitaśānti Stava

sources.title: Wisdomlib — 'Dhanta / Dhaṃta' (Prakrit↔Sanskrit: dhaṃta = dhmāta/dhvānta)
sources.consultedFor: Verifying dhaṁta = Skt. dhmāta ('fire-blown, smelted'), supporting 'refined silver'.

sources.title: Wisdomlib — 'Ruppa' (rūpya, silver)
sources.consultedFor: Verifying ruppa < Skt. rūpya ('wrought silver') for dhaṁta-ruppa.

sources.title: Wisdomlib — 'Samāhi' (Sheth, Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo: samāhi = samādhi)
sources.consultedFor: Verifying samāhi = Skt. samādhi for the closing petition 'païsa me samāhiṁ'.

sources.title: Wisdomlib — 'Dhavala' (white, bright)
sources.consultedFor: Confirming dhavala = 'dazzling white', capping the whiteness imagery.

notes: Book orthography preserved verbatim (doubled conjuncts haṭṭha/tuṭṭha/jiṭṭha, anusvāra in caṁda/saṁti). Key ambiguities: (1) haṭṭha-tuṭṭha may qualify the joyful venerators or the serene Jina — rendered to allow both. (2) મુત્તિ (mutti) is read as mukti ('liberation'), but some recensions read mūrti ('form'). (3) The final imperative is પઇસ (païsa, 'grant' < pradiśa); widely attested variants read pasia/pasiya (prasīda, 'be gracious') — the book's reading is followed and the variant flagged. (4) પટ્ટસેય seya is taken as sveta ('white'); a sveda parse exists but the whiteness sense is uncontested. Dictionary glosses verified via Cologne and Sheth's Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo (through Wisdomlib); the full per-verse English translation of this specific stanza was reconstructed from the lexical evidence, not copied from a single edition.
